Fix REAL defects in a generated HLD session. Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/ec6fef8d-7160-482a-8357-612b13ca5360_hld/_response.md (+ maybe capacity.py). Fix EACH directly in _response.md (and capacity.py if math is wrong, re-running with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python): 1. [MAJOR] §8 deep dive, line 634 (also propagated to NFR3 line 60, §6.4 lines 141/411/414/416/615, §9 lines 697/705/721, §11 lines 791/793/850): The failover-time math is internally contradictory and overclaims on the single most important number of the problem (LB-as-SPOF failover speed). Line 634 states: '1s heartbeat x 3 missed beats = ~3s detection, + sub-second VIP claim = ~2-3s failover, well under our NFR3 of ~2s.' This is false twice: (a) ~2-3s is not 'well under ~2s' — it meets or exceeds the budget; (b) the derived 3s detection ALONE already blows the ~2s NFR3 target before the VIP claim is added. The rescue clause ('if we tighten to ~3 missed beats at sub-second intervals') does not match the stated 1s-interval arithmetic. Meanwhile NFR3 (line 60) and all of §6.4 commit to a hard '~2s' / 'under a second' / 'about two seconds', but §9 and §11 silently quote '~2-3s' — so the doc asserts it satisfies a failover budget it actually breaches. The whole emphasis of this problem is the LB as SPOF and failover latency, so a self-inconsistent, mathematically-wrong claim on exactly that number is a real defect. FIX: Pick one consistent failover budget and make the math support it. Either (a) loosen NFR3 to '~2-3s' and align §6.4 (it currently says ~2s), OR (b) keep ≤2s and change the derivation to sub-second heartbeat intervals (e.g. 250ms x 3 = ~0.75s detection + sub-second claim ≈ ~1.5s). Then delete the false 'well under our NFR3 of ~2s' phrase on line 634 and reconcile every failover-time mention (lines 60, 141, 411, 414, 416, 615, 634, 639, 697, 705, 721, 791, 793, 850) to the same figure. CRITICAL for any mermaid finding: rewrite offending node/edge labels to remove ALL breaking characters (parens, colons, semicolons, slashes, quotes, angle/curly braces) — replace ":" and ";" with words, drop parens, use
for line breaks; keep flowchart TD. Keep all 11 sections, md > 20,000 chars, no LaTeX, scope-lock-first §1. Re-scan mermaid after fixing. Return what changed, final mdChars, final mermaidCount.

1	# Design a Load Balancer (10M requests/day)
2	
3	> The core tension here is unusual and worth naming in the first breath: at 10M requests a day the *traffic* is tiny — about a thousand requests a second — so this is **not a throughput problem, it's an availability problem.** The load balancer is the one box every request passes through, so the whole design is about making that box not be a single point of failure, detecting dead backends fast, and draining traffic gracefully — not about raw QPS.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the data plane and control plane, draw the high-level design one capability at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — health checking, the LB itself as a single point of failure, and connection draining — checking in with you as I go. This is an Amazon-style bar so I'll keep cost and operations first-class. Sound good?"
10	
11	> 💬 "Before I draw anything, let me lock down four assumptions that genuinely fork the architecture — stop me if any are wrong, because each one changes what I build."
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (assumptions stated FIRST — veto any of these)
14	
15	These are the four questions whose answers change the *shape* of the design. I'm stating each as a decision I'm making, not an open question:
16	
17	1. **I'll assume we're building the load balancer itself (the data plane + control plane), not just configuring a managed one (ALB/NLB).** That's the interesting version — if you actually want "pick AWS ALB and move on," that's a 30-second answer and we should talk about something else.
18	2. **I'll assume we need BOTH L4 and L7 modes, deployed as a two-tier design** — a thin L4 layer in front of a smarter L7 layer — because the problem brief explicitly calls out L4 vs L7. *(L4 = layer 4, routes by IP and port without looking inside the request; L7 = layer 7, reads the HTTP request and can route by URL path, cookie, or header.)*
19	3. **I'll assume HTTP/HTTPS traffic with keep-alive** *(one TCP connection carries many requests instead of one)*, so the connection rate is far lower than the request rate. If this were raw TCP with one request per connection, the connection-churn math changes a lot.
20	4. **I'll assume sticky sessions are NEEDED for some routes but the system should prefer stateless backends.** Stickiness *(sending the same client back to the same backend so in-memory session state is found)* is a correctness requirement only where backends hold session state; I'll support it but treat it as a fallback, not the default.
21	
22	> 🤝 "Here's what I'm treating as in scope and out of scope — add or cut before I commit. In scope: the data plane forwarding, health checking and ejection, L4 and L7 modes, the load-balancing algorithm, no single point of failure, sticky sessions, connection draining, and back-pressure. Out of scope below the line. Good?"
23	
24	### Functional requirements (the core flows — derived from the assumptions above)
25	
26	Since we're building the LB itself and need both tiers with health checking, the core flows are:
27	
28	**FR1 — Forward an incoming request to a healthy backend.** A client connection arrives at the LB and lands on a live backend chosen by the algorithm.
29	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when your browser hits our service, something in the middle picks one of the many identical servers behind it and hands your request off — and it never picks a dead one.
30	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the LB is on the path of *every single request*, so any latency or failure it adds is multiplied across the whole fleet, and it must never become the bottleneck or the outage.
31	
32	**FR2 — Health-check backends and eject dead ones automatically.** The LB continuously probes each backend and stops sending traffic to any that fails.
33	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the load balancer keeps quietly asking each server "are you alive?" and the moment one stops answering, it stops sending people there — no human paged.
34	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you must detect death fast enough to stop hurting users but slow enough that one slow probe doesn't eject a healthy server and cascade the whole fleet offline.
35	
36	**FR3 — Choose a backend by a configurable algorithm (round-robin, least-connections, consistent-hash).** The operator picks how traffic is spread.
37	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** sometimes you want to deal cards evenly, sometimes give work to whoever's least busy, sometimes always send the same customer to the same till.
38	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** least-connections needs shared real-time load state across LB nodes, and consistent-hash must keep sticky mappings stable when backends come and go.
39	
40	**FR4 — Survive losing any single LB node with no client-visible outage (no SPOF).** If an LB box dies, traffic keeps flowing.
41	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the thing whose job is to prevent outages can't itself be the thing that causes one — if it dies, a twin takes over instantly.
42	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the LB *is* the front door; making the front door redundant means a virtual IP that can move between machines in under a second.
43	
44	**FR5 — Drain connections gracefully on backend removal/deploy.** When a backend is taken out, in-flight requests finish before it's killed.
45	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when we retire a server, we stop sending it new customers but let the ones already inside finish checking out before we lock the door.
46	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you need to distinguish "no new connections" from "kill existing ones," and bound how long you'll wait so a stuck request can't block a deploy forever.
47	
48	**Below the line (out of scope):** TLS certificate issuance/rotation (assume we terminate TLS but cert management is a separate system); WAF / DDoS scrubbing (upstream of us); global DNS-based geo-routing (we're regional; I'll mention anycast for multi-region); the backend application logic itself; autoscaling the backend fleet (we react to it, we don't drive it).
49	
50	### Non-functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets — tagged to assumptions)
51	
52	**NFR1 — Availability ≥ 99.99% on the forwarding path** (four nines).
53	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** four nines means under ~52 minutes of total downtime per year for the front door.
54	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the LB is on every request, so its downtime is the *whole service's* downtime — if the LB is down, nothing behind it is reachable even if every backend is perfectly healthy.
55	
56	**NFR2 — Added latency p99 ≤ ~1ms at L4, ≤ ~5ms at L7** (ASSUMING regional, same-AZ backends).
57	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the load balancer itself should add at most a few milliseconds — you shouldn't be able to feel that it's there.
58	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the LB tax is paid on every request; 20ms here is 20ms added to every user interaction in the company.
59	
60	**NFR3 — Failover (VIP move) ≤ ~2 seconds; dead-backend ejection ≤ ~6 seconds.**
61	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if an LB box dies its twin takes over in about two seconds; if a backend dies we stop using it within about six.
62	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** slow ejection means thousands of requests get routed into a black hole and time out before we react.
63	
64	**NFR4 — Correctness of stickiness where enabled** (ASSUMING some stateful backends).
65	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if a route needs the same server every time, it gets the same server — even as backends are added or removed.
66	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a user's shopping cart or login session vanishes mid-flow because request two landed on a different backend than request one.
67	
68	> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** least-connections across multiple active LB nodes needs *shared* connection-count state, which is itself a small coordination problem — I'll address it in the deep dives rather than pretend it's free.
69	
70	> 🎙️ **Script:** "So the job is: forward every request to a healthy backend, health-check and eject the dead ones automatically, support a few balancing algorithms including sticky sessions, and — the big one — never be a single point of failure, with graceful draining on deploys. The defining requirement isn't throughput, it's availability: this box is on the path of every request, so four nines on the LB *is* four nines on the whole product. I'll keep cost and ops first-class since this is an Amazon-style bar."
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
75	
76	The three assumptions from §1 that most change the architecture if I guessed wrong:
77	
78	**Fork 1 — I locked "build it ourselves, L4 + L7 two-tier."**
79	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
80	  - If you actually want a *managed* LB → the whole design collapses to "pick NLB for L4 / ALB for L7, set health-check params, done" and we should instead go deep on something like the backend itself.
81	  - If you want L7 *only* (no L4 tier) → I drop the IPVS layer and accept a higher per-node cost and lower max throughput ceiling, which is fine at 10M/day but caps our headroom.
82	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm building the real thing with both layers — if you'd rather I assume managed and spend the time elsewhere, say so."
83	
84	**Fork 2 — I locked "HTTP with keep-alive, so connection rate is low."**
85	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
86	  - If it's actually one-request-per-connection (no keep-alive) → connection-churn dominates, the L4 layer's connections-per-second becomes the binding metric, and SYN-flood-style overload protection moves up the priority list.
87	  - If it's long-lived connections (websockets, gRPC streams) → least-connections beats round-robin badly, and connection draining gets much harder because connections live for minutes.
88	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming normal web keep-alive traffic — if these are long-lived streams or websockets, the balancing algorithm and draining story both change and I'd lead with that."
89	
90	**Fork 3 — I locked "single region; multi-region is anycast later."**
91	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
92	  - If single region is fine → VIP failover via a floating virtual IP is the whole no-SPOF story.
93	  - If we need multi-region → I add anycast *(the same IP announced from many locations, so the network routes you to the nearest one)* plus DNS health-based failover, and the active-passive VIP becomes a per-region detail.
94	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm regional to start; if you want global, I layer anycast on top and the VIP story becomes per-region — tell me and I'll draw that."
95	
96	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three places my design is most sensitive. Want me to proceed with build-it-yourself, regional, keep-alive HTTP — or redirect any of them before I draw?"
97	
98	---
99	
100	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
101	
102	I computed these with a small script so the arithmetic is exact; I'll only say the one or two numbers out loud that actually drive a decision. The punchline up front: **the load is tiny — the design is driven by availability and failover speed, not throughput.**
103	
104	> 🧠 *Glossary as we go:* **RPS** = requests per second. **CPS** = new connections per second (the L4 metric — with keep-alive, many requests share one connection). **Burst factor** = peak ÷ average; web traffic is spiky so I use 10x. **Egress** = bytes we send back out to clients (the part cloud providers charge for).
105	
106	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
107	|---|---|---|---|---|
108	| Average RPS | 10,000,000 ÷ 86,400 s = 115.7 | ~115/s | "about a hundred requests a second" | Confirms this is small — one node could do it. |
109	| Peak RPS | 115.7 × 10 burst = 1,157 | ~1.2K/s | "a bit over a thousand a second at peak" | Still tiny — sizing is for HA, not load. |
110	| Peak CPS (L4) | 1,157 RPS ÷ 50 keep-alive = 23 | ~23/s | "around twenty new connections a second" | L4 layer is nearly idle — not the bottleneck. |
111	| Peak egress | 1,157/s × 30 KB × 8 = 0.28 Gbps | ~0.3 Gbps | "under a third of a gigabit" | Fits one NIC easily; egress is the real $ line. |
112	| Monthly egress | 10M/day × 30 KB × 30 days = 9 TB | ~9 TB/mo | "about nine terabytes a month" | ~$450/mo egress — biggest variable cost. |
113	| **L7 node ceiling vs load** | one L7 proxy ~50K RPS; we need 1,157 → **0.023 of a node** | **~2% of one node** | **"we use about two percent of a single box"** | **THE decision number — see below.** |
114	| L4 node ceiling | one IPVS node ~300K CPS; we need 23 | <0.01% | "rounding error" | L4 is free capacity-wise. |
115	| Health-check load | 3 LB nodes × 200 backends ÷ 2s = 300 probes/s; each backend sees 1.5/s | ~300/s total | "negligible probe traffic" | Active checks don't cost anything meaningful. |
116	| Ejection detect time | 3 failures × 2s interval = 6s | ~6s | "dead backend gone in six seconds" | Tunes the health-check threshold (FR2/NFR3). |
117	| Conn-table memory | 20K concurrent conns × 300 B = 6 MB | ~6 MB | "a few megabytes of state" | State is trivial; no memory pressure. |
118	
119	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** we use **~2% of a single L7 node** at peak. That means **node count is dictated entirely by availability, not throughput** — we don't add nodes for load, we add them for redundancy. So the answer is "the minimum number for no-SPOF," which is **two active-passive nodes per AZ across two AZs** (4 total in the data path), sized far below their ceiling so a peer's death never cascades.
120	
121	**The flip threshold:** if peak RPS ever crosses **~25K/s** (one node's 50K ceiling at our 50% headroom rule), throughput starts to matter and we move from "2 nodes for HA" to "N nodes for load, horizontally scaled behind ECMP/anycast." We're 20x below that today.
122	
123	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The numbers tell a clear story — peak is about twelve hundred requests a second, which is roughly two percent of what one proxy box can handle. So I'm not sizing for load at all; one node would do it forty times over. I'm sizing for *availability* — the LB is on every request, so I need redundancy, not horsepower. The only number that flips that is if we ever cross about twenty-five thousand a second, and we're nowhere near it. The real money is egress — about nine terabytes a month — not compute."
124	
125	---
126	
127	## 4. Core Entities
128	
129	> 💬 "These are first-draft entities — the nouns of the system. I'll give full field definitions in the data model in section 7."
130	
131	**Backend (Target)** — one server in the pool that can serve requests; owns its address, weight, and current health state.
132	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** one open checkout lane in a supermarket — it has a number, it's either open or closed, and we can route shoppers to it.
133	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "how do you know a backend is healthy?" → it passes a configurable number of consecutive health checks; failing the threshold flips it to ejected and it stops receiving new connections.
134	
135	**Backend Pool (Target Group)** — the set of interchangeable backends for one service, plus the policy (algorithm, health-check config) for that set.
136	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** all the checkout lanes for one store, plus the manager's rule for how to send shoppers to them.
137	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "how do backends join and leave?" → the control plane reconciles desired vs actual membership; adds start in a slow-start ramp, removals go through draining first.
138	
139	**Listener / Virtual IP (VIP)** — the public-facing endpoint clients connect to (an IP + port, e.g. the front door); owns the binding to a pool and the L4-or-L7 mode.
140	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** the store's single street address that every customer drives to, no matter which lane they end up in.
141	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "what happens to the VIP when the LB node holding it dies?" → it's a *floating* IP; a peer claims it via failover (VRRP/keepalived) in ~2s, so the address stays reachable.
142	
143	**Health Checker** — the control-plane component that probes backends and publishes the health map.
144	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** the floor supervisor who keeps walking the lanes asking "you good?" and radioing back which lanes to stop using.
145	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "what if the checker itself is wrong (false positive)?" → each LB node checks independently and we require a quorum / multiple consecutive failures before ejection so one flaky probe path can't down a healthy backend.
146	
147	**Connection / Session** — an in-flight client connection and (if sticky) its mapping to a chosen backend.
148	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** a shopper currently in a specific lane — and, if we promised it, the note that says "always send *this* shopper to lane 4."
149	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "what happens to live connections during a deploy?" → draining: the backend is marked "no new connections" but existing ones finish until a drain timeout.
150	
151	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The nouns are simple: a Backend is one server, a Pool is the set of identical servers for a service, the VIP is the single front-door address clients hit, the Health Checker is the supervisor watching the backends, and a Connection is one in-flight request flow. The one to watch is the VIP — it has to be a *floating* address that can jump to a healthy node, because that's the whole no-single-point-of-failure story. I'll flesh out fields in the data model."
152	
153	---
154	
155	## 5. API / Interface
156	
157	The LB has two interfaces: a **control-plane management API** (how operators configure it) and the **data plane** (the actual forwarding, which isn't a REST call — it's packet/connection handling). I'll define the management API per requirement and describe the data-plane contract separately.
158	
159	> Cross-cutting (stated once): **Versioning** — all management endpoints are prefixed `/v1`; additive changes (a new optional field) don't bump the version, breaking changes get `/v2`. **Pagination** — list endpoints (backends, pools) use cursor/keyset tokens, not offset/limit, because the backend set changes under you and offset paging would skip or duplicate rows. **Security** — caller identity comes from a signed JWT/mTLS cert, never a client-supplied id; object-level authz checks the caller owns the pool they're editing ("can this operator modify THIS pool?"), not just "is the caller logged in."
160	
161	**Pool management (serves FR3 — algorithm config)**
162	- **PUT /v1/pools/{poolId}** — PUT because we fully replace the pool's configuration (idempotent by construction).
163	- Key fields: `algorithm: enum` *(round_robin | least_conn | consistent_hash — the balancing policy)*; `stickiness: object` *(cookie name + TTL, present only if FR4 stickiness is needed)*; `healthCheck: object` *(path, interval, thresholds)*. The client does **not** send backend health — that's server-derived from probes; a client can't claim a backend is healthy.
164	
165	```
166	// Request
167	PUT /v1/pools/web-frontend
168	{ "algorithm": "least_conn",
169	  "healthCheck": { "path": "/healthz", "intervalSec": 2, "unhealthyThreshold": 3, "healthyThreshold": 2 },
170	  "stickiness": { "type": "cookie", "ttlSec": 3600 } }
171	
172	// Response 200
173	{ "poolId": "web-frontend", "algorithm": "least_conn", "backendCount": 200, "version": 47 }
174	```
175	- 🔑 **Key decision:** PUT is idempotent — replaying the same config is a no-op, so a retried control-plane call can't corrupt state. The `version` is an optimistic-concurrency token returned so the next edit can detect a conflicting concurrent change.
176	
177	**Register / deregister a backend (serves FR1, FR5 — membership + draining)**
178	- **POST /v1/pools/{poolId}/backends** to add; **DELETE /v1/pools/{poolId}/backends/{backendId}?drain=true** to remove gracefully.
179	- Key fields on POST: `address: string` *(ip:port)*; `weight: int` *(relative share for weighted algorithms)*. On DELETE, `drain=true` and `drainTimeoutSec` control graceful removal (FR5).
180	
181	```
182	// Request
183	POST /v1/pools/web-frontend/backends
184	{ "address": "10.0.3.14:8080", "weight": 100 }
185	
186	// Response 201
187	{ "backendId": "be_9f2", "state": "STARTING", "address": "10.0.3.14:8080" }
188	```
189	- 🔑 **Key decision:** a newly added backend returns `STARTING`, not `HEALTHY` — it must pass `healthyThreshold` checks AND ramp through slow-start before it gets full traffic, so a cold backend isn't instantly buried.
190	
191	**Read health / status (serves FR2 — observability of ejection)**
192	- **GET /v1/pools/{poolId}/backends?cursor=...** — GET, read-only, cursor-paginated.
193	```
194	// GET /v1/pools/web-frontend/backends
195	// Response 200
196	{ "backends": [
197	    { "backendId": "be_9f2", "address": "10.0.3.14:8080", "state": "HEALTHY", "activeConns": 312 },
198	    { "backendId": "be_1a7", "address": "10.0.3.15:8080", "state": "EJECTED", "lastFailReason": "timeout" } ],
199	  "nextCursor": null }
200	```
201	- 🔑 **Key decision:** `state` and `activeConns` are server-owned and read-only here — operators observe, they don't set health by hand (an override is a separate explicit "maintenance" mutation, audited).
202	
203	**Data-plane contract (serves FR1 — the actual forwarding, not REST)**
204	- The client opens a TCP connection to the **VIP**. The LB does NOT speak a request/response management protocol here — it forwards. At **L4** it rewrites/forwards packets to a chosen backend with no body inspection; at **L7** it terminates the connection, parses the HTTP request, applies path/header routing, then proxies to a backend over a (pooled, keep-alive) upstream connection. Trace context (`X-Request-Id`) is injected at L7 so one request is traceable end to end.
205	
206	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The API splits in two. There's a small control-plane REST API operators use — PUT a pool's algorithm and health-check config, POST and DELETE backends with a drain flag, GET the live health. Everything that matters — health state, active connections — is server-owned and read-only to callers; you can't lie to it. The data plane isn't REST at all: clients just open a connection to one front-door VIP, and at L4 we forward packets blindly while at L7 we actually read the HTTP request and route on it. I version everything under v1 and check the operator owns the pool before any edit."
207	
208	---
209	
210	## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)
211	
212	I'll define the color language once and reuse it: blue = clients, green = our services (data plane), amber = control-plane/async, grey = state stores.
213	
214	### 6.1 "Forward an incoming request to a healthy backend" (FR1)
215	
216	The foundational slice: a client hits the VIP and lands on a live backend. I'll introduce the two-tier data plane here.
217	
218	**Architecture decisions table**
219	
220	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
221	|---|---|---|---|---|
222	| L4 layer (IPVS) | A kernel-level packet forwarder that routes by IP+port without reading the request | Handles huge connection volume cheaply and survives an L7 node death by spreading across the L7 pool | Not L7-only: every node would parse + terminate TLS, capping throughput and raising cost for no benefit at our scale | Can't route on URL/cookie — that's the L7 tier's job |
223	| L7 layer (Envoy) | A proxy that terminates the connection, reads the HTTP request, and routes on path/header/cookie | Need path-based routing, TLS termination, stickiness, retries — only an L7 proxy does this | Not HAProxy-only: Envoy has a clean xDS control-plane API and better observability for dynamic config | More CPU per request than L4; mitigated by L4 fan-out |
224	| VIP (floating IP) | One public address clients connect to that can move between nodes | Single stable entry point that's also movable for failover (FR4) | Not per-node IPs + client-side LB: pushes routing into every client, brittle to change | Failover machinery (VRRP) needed to move it |
225	| Backend pool | The set of identical app servers behind the LB | Stateless, horizontally interchangeable so any algorithm can pick any one | n/a — given | Backends must be (mostly) stateless for non-sticky routes |
226	
227	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "I'm using two tiers. The front tier is L4 — it's a kernel-level forwarder, IPVS, that just looks at IP and port and shoves the connection at one of the L7 nodes; it's nearly free and absurdly fast. Behind it is the L7 tier, Envoy, which actually terminates the connection, reads the HTTP, and does the smart routing — path-based rules, stickiness, TLS termination, retries. I picked Envoy over HAProxy mainly because it has a clean dynamic-config API, xDS, which matters when backends churn. The clients only ever see one address, the VIP. The cost is the L7 tier burns more CPU per request, but the L4 fan-out and our tiny load make that a non-issue."
228	
229	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
230	> **L4 (Layer 4) load balancing** — routing by network address and port without opening the request. Here it's the cheap, fast front door that fans connections across the L7 nodes.
231	> **L7 (Layer 7) load balancing** — routing by the contents of the HTTP request (URL, headers, cookies). Here it's where path-based routing, stickiness, and TLS termination happen.
232	> **IPVS** — a load balancer built into the Linux kernel that forwards packets very fast. Here it's the L4 tier so the front door can't be a throughput bottleneck.
233	> **Envoy** — a modern proxy with a live configuration API. Here it's the L7 tier so we can add/remove backends without restarting.
234	> **VIP (Virtual IP)** — one IP address that isn't permanently tied to one machine and can be claimed by another. Here it's the single client-facing front door that can move for failover.
235	> **TLS termination** — decrypting HTTPS at the LB so backends speak plain HTTP. Here it centralizes certificates at the L7 tier.
236

242	    Client -->|"1 connect to VIP"| L4
243	    subgraph DataPlane["Data plane"]

245	        L4 -->|"2 forward connection"| L7
246	        L7[L7 tier Envoy]:::svc
247	        L7 -->|"3 pick healthy backend"| BE1[Backend A]:::svc
248	        L7 -->|"3 pick healthy backend"| BE2[Backend B]:::svc
249	        L7 -->|"3 pick healthy backend"| BE3[Backend C]:::svc
250	    end
251	    HealthMap[(Health map)]:::store
252	    HealthMap -.->|"4 who is alive"| L7
253	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3

257	↳ reuses existing: none — this is the first slice.
258	
259	**Numbered step narration:**
260	1. **Client connects to the VIP.** The client only knows one address. *Why here:* the VIP is the single stable entry point so clients are decoupled from individual machines. *State change:* a new TCP connection exists at the L4 tier. *What could go wrong:* if the node owning the VIP is dead, see FR4 — the VIP floats to a peer.
261	2. **L4 forwards the connection to an L7 node.** IPVS picks an L7 node by a simple hash so the same connection stays pinned. *Why here:* L4 is cheap and spreads load across L7 nodes without parsing anything. *State change:* connection now pinned to one L7 node. *What could go wrong:* an L7 node dies mid-connection → the client retries, L4 re-hashes to a live node.
262	3. **L7 picks a healthy backend by the configured algorithm.** It consults the health map and applies round-robin/least-conn/consistent-hash. *Why here:* only L7 has the request context and health view to choose well. *State change:* request is proxied to a specific backend; its active-conn count increments. *What could go wrong:* chosen backend just died → L7 retries the request on another backend (idempotent reads only; see deep dives).
263	4. **L7 reads the health map** (continuously, not per-request). *Why here:* the health map is the source of truth for "who's alive," kept fresh by FR2's checker. *State change:* none — read-only. *What could go wrong:* stale map → could pick a just-died backend; bounded by ejection time (~6s) and per-request retry.
264	
265	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Two tiers — the dumb-fast L4 front door fans connections to the smart L7 layer, and the L7 layer is the only thing that actually reads the request and consults who's alive before it picks a backend."
266	
267	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A request comes in to the single VIP, the L4 tier blindly fans it to an L7 node, and the L7 node is the brain — it reads the HTTP, checks the live health map, and picks a backend by whatever algorithm is configured. The key decision is the two-tier split: keep the front door dumb and fast so it never bottlenecks, and put all the intelligence one layer back where it's cheap to scale. The trade-off is an extra hop, but at our latency budget that's well under our few-millisecond target."
268	
269	### 6.2 "Health-check backends and eject dead ones" (FR2)
270	
271	This slice adds the control plane that keeps the health map honest. New component: the Health Checker.
272	
273	**Architecture decisions table**
274	
275	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
276	|---|---|---|---|---|
277	| Active health checker | A control-plane probe that periodically calls each backend's /healthz | Detects death even with zero live traffic; deterministic timing | Not passive-only: passive (watching real request errors) misses idle backends and is slow to react | Adds probe traffic — but we computed it's negligible (~1.5/s per backend) |
278	| Passive outlier detection | The L7 proxy ejects a backend that returns errors/timeouts on real traffic | Catches failures the active probe misses (e.g. backend healthy on /healthz but failing real requests) | Not active-only: a backend can pass /healthz while failing real work | Can over-eject under a thundering retry storm — bounded by min-healthy floor |
279	| Health map | A small shared store of each backend's state, versioned | Single source of truth all L7 nodes read so they agree on who's alive | Not per-node independent maps with no sharing: nodes would disagree and flap | Must be highly available and fast to read — kept in memory + gossip |
280	
281	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Health checking is two halves. Active checks are the control plane calling each backend's health endpoint on a timer — that catches a dead backend even if no real traffic is hitting it. Passive checks are the L7 proxy watching real requests and ejecting a backend that starts erroring even though it still answers /healthz. I want both because each catches what the other misses. They both feed one shared, versioned health map that every L7 node reads, so the nodes never disagree about who's alive. The risk is over-ejection during a retry storm, so I enforce a minimum-healthy floor — I'll never eject below it."
282	
283	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
284	> **Active health check** — the LB proactively pinging a backend's `/healthz` on a schedule. Here it detects dead backends even with no live traffic.
285	> **Passive health check (outlier detection)** — watching real request results and ejecting a backend that errors. Here it catches backends that lie about being healthy.
286	> **Health map** — the shared record of each backend's state. Here it's the one truth all L7 nodes consult before routing.
287	> **Ejection** — temporarily removing a backend from the rotation. Here it's reversible: the backend rejoins after passing `healthyThreshold` checks.
288	
289	**Diagram — health check + ejection loop:**
290	
291	```mermaid
292	flowchart TD
293	    HC[Health checker]:::async
294	    HC -->|"1 probe healthz every 2s"| BE1[Backend A]:::svc
295	    HC -->|"1 probe healthz every 2s"| BE2[Backend B]:::svc
296	    BE1 -->|"2 200 OK"| HC
297	    BE2 -->|"3 timeout x3"| HC
298	    HC -->|"4 mark ejected"| HealthMap[(Health map)]:::store
299	    L7[L7 tier Envoy]:::svc
300	    L7 -->|"5 passive errors"| HealthMap
301	    HealthMap -.->|"6 skip ejected"| L7
302	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
303	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
304	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
305	```
306	↳ reuses existing: L7 tier and Health map (from §6.1).
307	
308	**Numbered step narration:**
309	1. **Checker probes each backend's /healthz every 2s.** *Why here:* a dedicated checker gives deterministic timing independent of traffic. *State:* per-backend success/fail counters updated. *What could go wrong:* the checker itself dies → each L7 node also runs its own checks (defense in depth), so no single checker is fatal.
310	2. **Backend A returns 200** → its healthy counter increments; stays in rotation. *State:* unchanged (already healthy).
311	3. **Backend B times out 3 times in a row** (the unhealthy threshold). *Why 3:* one blip shouldn't eject; three consecutive is real. *State:* B crosses the threshold. *What could go wrong:* a network blip between checker and B but B is fine for clients → mitigated by passive checks confirming and by the min-healthy floor.
312	4. **Checker marks B EJECTED in the health map** (version bumps). *State:* health map now says B is out. *What could go wrong:* if this would drop us below min-healthy backends, we refuse and alarm instead — better to send to a degraded backend than to none.
313	5. **L7 also feeds passive signals** — real request errors/timeouts on a backend push it toward ejection. *State:* corroborates or independently triggers ejection.
314	6. **L7 reads the updated map and skips B.** New requests avoid B within one map-propagation cycle (~sub-second). B is re-probed and rejoins after `healthyThreshold` successes.
315	
316	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Active checks catch the dead, passive checks catch the lying — both write one shared health map, and I never eject below a minimum-healthy floor so a bad probe can't black-hole the whole fleet."
317	
318	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I run active checks — the control plane pinging /healthz every couple seconds — plus passive outlier detection where the proxy ejects a backend that errors on real traffic. Three consecutive failures ejects, two successes re-admits, so we react in about six seconds without flapping on a single blip. Both write one shared, versioned health map every L7 node reads. The one safety rail I insist on is a minimum-healthy floor: I will never eject a backend if it would drop the pool below that floor, because routing to a struggling backend beats routing to nothing."
319	
320	### 6.3 "Choose a backend by a configurable algorithm" (FR3)
321	
322	This slice adds the algorithm logic inside L7. No new infra — it adds the load-state store needed for least-connections.
323	
324	**Architecture decisions table**
325	
326	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
327	|---|---|---|---|---|
328	| Round-robin selector | Deal requests evenly in rotation | Default — simplest, fair when backends and requests are uniform | Not random: round-robin is more even with small fleets | Ignores backend load — a slow backend still gets its turn |
329	| Least-connections selector | Send to the backend with the fewest active connections | Best when request durations vary a lot (some slow, some fast) | Not round-robin for long requests: RR piles up on a stuck backend | Needs shared/approximate conn counts across L7 nodes (see §8) |
330	| Consistent-hash selector | Map a key (cookie/IP) to a backend on a hash ring so the same key sticks | Powers stickiness; minimizes reshuffling when backends change | Not modulo-N hashing: adding one backend remaps almost everything | Slight imbalance; fixed with virtual nodes on the ring |
331	
332	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
333	> **Round-robin** — hand out requests in turn like dealing cards. Here it's the safe default.
334	> **Least-connections** — pick whoever's least busy right now. Here it shines when request times vary.
335	> **Consistent hashing** — a hash ring where each key lands on the next backend clockwise, so adding/removing one backend only moves a small slice of keys. Here it underpins sticky sessions without mass reshuffling.
336	> **Virtual nodes** — placing each backend at many points on the ring. Here they smooth out imbalance.
337	
338	**Diagram — algorithm selection inside L7:**
339	
340	```mermaid
341	flowchart TD
342	    Req[Incoming request]:::client
343	    Req -->|"1 enter selector"| Sel{Algorithm}:::svc
344	    Sel -->|"2a round robin"| RR[Next in rotation]:::svc
345	    Sel -->|"2b least conn"| LC[Fewest active conns]:::svc
346	    Sel -->|"2c consistent hash"| CH[Hash key to ring]:::svc
347	    LC -.->|"3 read counts"| LoadState[(Conn-count state)]:::store
348	    RR -->|"4 chosen backend"| BE[Backend]:::svc
349	    LC -->|"4 chosen backend"| BE
350	    CH -->|"4 chosen backend"| BE
351	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
352	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
353	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
354	```
355	↳ reuses existing: L7 tier, Backend (from §6.1).
356	
357	**Numbered step narration:**
358	1. **Request enters the selector** configured for the pool. *State:* none yet.
359	2. **The configured algorithm runs.** Round-robin just advances a counter; least-conn reads connection counts; consistent-hash hashes the sticky key onto the ring. *Why:* operator chose the policy that fits the workload.
360	3. **Least-conn reads the connection-count state.** *What could go wrong:* across multiple L7 nodes, exact global counts are expensive — so we use *power-of-two-choices* (pick two backends at random, send to the less loaded), which is near-optimal without global coordination. Detailed in §8.
361	4. **The chosen backend is returned** and the request is proxied; its active-conn count increments (and decrements on completion). *What could go wrong:* the chosen backend is unhealthy → the selector excludes ejected backends from candidacy by reading the health map first.
362	
363	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Round-robin is the safe default, least-connections wins when request times vary — but exact global counts are costly, so I use power-of-two-choices — and consistent hashing is what makes stickiness survive backends coming and going."
364	
365	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three algorithms. Round-robin is the default — deal evenly. Least-connections is better when some requests are slow, but tracking exact connection counts across all the L7 nodes is expensive, so I use power-of-two-choices: pick two backends at random and send to the less busy one — that's provably almost as good with zero coordination. Consistent hashing is the one that powers stickiness — it's a ring so that adding or removing a backend only reshuffles a small slice of keys, not everyone. I sprinkle virtual nodes on the ring to keep it balanced."
366	
367	### 6.4 "Survive losing any single LB node — no SPOF" (FR4)
368	
369	The defining slice. New mechanism: floating VIP with active-passive failover, replicated across AZs.
370	
371	**Architecture decisions table**
372	
373	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
374	|---|---|---|---|---|
375	| Floating VIP + VRRP | A virtual IP that a backup node claims via a protocol if the master stops sending heartbeats | Sub-2s failover, no DNS TTL wait, clients unaware | Not DNS failover alone: DNS TTLs cache for minutes — far too slow | One node is passive (idle) — wasted capacity, fine at our scale |
376	| Multi-AZ deployment | LB nodes split across two data centres in the region | Survives a whole-AZ power/network loss | Not single-AZ: one building fire = total outage | Cross-AZ traffic costs a little; worth it |
377	| ECMP / anycast (scale-out) | Router spreads the VIP across many active nodes via equal-cost paths | Needed only above ~25K RPS to go active-active | Not now: active-passive is simpler and we're 20x under the threshold | Deferred until load demands it |
378	
379	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
380	> **VRRP (keepalived)** — a protocol where a backup node watches the master's heartbeat and grabs the shared IP if it stops. Here it moves the VIP in ~2s with no client change.
381	> **Multi-AZ** — multiple independent data centres in one region. Here it means one building dying doesn't take us down.
382	> **Anycast / ECMP** — announcing the same IP from many places so routers spread traffic. Here it's the future active-active path if load grows.
383	> **Gratuitous ARP** — a broadcast that says "this IP now lives at my MAC address." Here it's how the new master makes the network send the VIP's traffic to it.
384	
385	**Diagram — VIP failover (no SPOF):**
386	
387	```mermaid
388	flowchart TD
389	    Client[Client app]:::client
390	    Client -->|"1 connect to VIP"| VIP{{Floating VIP}}:::svc
391	    subgraph AZ1["AZ-1"]
392	        Master[LB master]:::svc
393	    end
394	    subgraph AZ2["AZ-2"]
395	        Backup[LB backup]:::svc
396	    end
397	    VIP -->|"2 normally points to master"| Master
398	    Master -.->|"3 VRRP heartbeat"| Backup
399	    Backup -.->|"4 heartbeat lost claim VIP"| VIP
400	    Master -->|"5 forward"| Pool[Backend pool]:::svc
401	    Backup -->|"5 forward after failover"| Pool
402	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
403	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
404	```
405	↳ reuses existing: Backend pool (from §6.1), VIP (from §6.1, now shown floating).
406	
407	**Numbered step narration:**
408	1. **Client connects to the VIP** as always — unaware of which physical node holds it.
409	2. **VIP normally resolves to the master** in AZ-1, which forwards to the pool. *State:* master is active, backup idle.
410	3. **Master sends VRRP heartbeats** to the backup every ~1s. *Why:* the backup needs a fast, reliable liveness signal.
411	4. **Backup misses heartbeats → claims the VIP** by broadcasting a gratuitous ARP so the network's switches send the VIP's traffic to the backup's MAC. *State:* backup is now master; failover in ~2s. *What could go wrong:* *split-brain* — both think they're master. Mitigated by a higher VRRP priority + a quorum/fencing check (deep dives).
412	5. **Traffic flows through the new master** to the same pool. In-flight connections on the dead node are lost (clients retry); new connections are unaffected.
413	
414	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The front door is a *floating* IP — if the node holding it dies, a backup grabs the same address in about two seconds via VRRP, so clients never change anything; the only thing we have to fight is split-brain."
415	
416	> 🎙️ **Script:** "This is the heart of the design. The VIP isn't bolted to one machine — it floats. The master holds it and heartbeats a standby; if those heartbeats stop, the standby claims the IP with a gratuitous ARP and traffic redirects in about two seconds, with clients none the wiser. I split master and standby across two availability zones so a whole data centre can die and we survive. The classic failure is split-brain — both nodes thinking they own the IP — which I handle with VRRP priorities and fencing. The cost is the standby sits idle, but at our load that waste is irrelevant, and it buys four nines."
417	
418	### 6.5 "Drain connections gracefully on removal/deploy" (FR5)
419	
420	Final slice. New mechanism: a draining state in the backend lifecycle.
421	
422	**Architecture decisions table**
423	
424	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
425	|---|---|---|---|---|
426	| Draining state | A backend state meaning "no new connections, finish existing ones" | Lets deploys/scale-down complete in-flight requests cleanly | Not hard kill: dropping live connections = user-visible errors mid-request | Deploys wait up to a drain timeout |
427	| Drain timeout | A bound (e.g. 30s) after which remaining connections are forcibly closed | Stops a stuck request blocking a deploy forever | Not infinite drain: one hung request could freeze a rollout | A few unlucky long requests get cut at the timeout |
428	| Slow-start (add side) | New backends ramp traffic up gradually | A cold backend with empty caches isn't instantly buried | Not full-traffic-on-join: cold backend would slow or fall over | Brief period of slightly uneven load |
429	
430	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
431	> **Connection draining** — stop sending new requests to a backend but let current ones finish. Here it makes deploys and scale-downs invisible to users.
432	> **Drain timeout** — the max time we wait for in-flight requests before force-closing. Here it bounds how long a deploy can stall.
433	> **Slow-start** — ramping a fresh backend's share of traffic up over time. Here it protects cold backends from a thundering herd.
434	
435	**Diagram — backend lifecycle (drain on removal):**
436	
437	```mermaid
438	flowchart TD
439	    Healthy[HEALTHY in rotation]:::svc
440	    Healthy -->|"1 deregister with drain"| Draining[DRAINING]:::async
441	    Draining -->|"2 no new conns finish old"| Wait[In-flight requests complete]:::svc
442	    Wait -->|"3 all done or timeout"| Removed[REMOVED]:::store
443	    Starting[STARTING slow-start]:::async
444	    Starting -->|"4 passes checks ramp up"| Healthy
445	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
446	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
447	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
448	```
449	↳ reuses existing: Backend states feed the Health map (from §6.2).
450	
451	**Numbered step narration:**
452	1. **Deregister with drain=true** moves the backend to DRAINING. *State:* L7 stops routing *new* connections to it. *Why:* clean shutdown for deploys.
453	2. **In-flight requests keep flowing** to the draining backend until they finish. *What could go wrong:* a long-running request → bounded by the drain timeout.
454	3. **When all connections close OR the drain timeout fires, the backend is REMOVED.** Forced close at timeout returns a clean connection close so the client can retry. *State:* backend fully out.
455	4. **On the add side, a STARTING backend ramps via slow-start** before becoming HEALTHY. *What could go wrong:* cold backend buried at full traffic → slow-start prevents it.
456	
457	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Removing a backend isn't a kill — it's DRAINING: no new connections, finish the ones inside, force-close at a timeout so a stuck request can't freeze a deploy."
458	
459	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Removing a backend goes through a draining state — we stop sending it new connections but let the in-flight ones finish, with a hard timeout, say thirty seconds, so one hung request can't block a rollout forever. The mirror image on the add side is slow-start: a fresh backend ramps up its share of traffic instead of getting buried while its caches are cold. The trade-off is deploys wait a bounded few seconds, which is exactly what you want — invisible to users."

490	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Putting it together: clients hit one floating VIP, the L4 tier fans connections to the L7 tier, and the L7 tier reads the health map and picks a backend by the configured algorithm. On the control side, the health checker probes backends and the proxy adds passive signals, both writing one shared health map that the data plane reads. A standby in a second AZ heartbeats the master and grabs the VIP if it dies. The whole thing is built around one idea — the box that prevents outages can't cause one."
491	
492	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The three richest places to go deep are health-checking without flapping, the LB-as-SPOF and split-brain, and graceful draining — which is most interesting to you?"

496	## 7. Data Model & Storage
497	
498	The LB is mostly *stateless on the data path* — the only real persistent state is configuration and the (small, fast, replicated) health/load state. There's no big growing table here; that's a defining property worth saying out loud.
499	
500	**PART A — Entity table**
501	
502	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
503	|---|---|---|---|---|
504	| Pool config | **poolId (PK)**, algorithm, healthCheck, stickiness, version | Replicated config store (etcd) | poolId | Strong (config must agree) |
505	| Backend | **backendId (PK)**, poolId, address, weight, state | Config store + in-memory health map | poolId | Strong for membership, eventual for live state |
506	| Health state | backendId, state, failCount, lastProbe | In-memory + gossip across LB nodes | backendId | Eventual (sub-second converge) |
507	| Conn-count state | backendId, activeConns | In-memory per-node + power-of-two approx | backendId | Approximate (intentionally) |
508	| Stickiness map | hashKey → backendId (on the ring) | Computed (consistent-hash ring) + cookie | hashKey | Deterministic from ring state |
509	
510	**PART B — Storage decision cards**
511	
512	**🗄️ etcd — used for: Pool config, Backend membership**
513	1. **What it is:** etcd is a small, strongly-consistent key-value store with built-in replication and watch — when a value changes, watchers are notified instantly.
514	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it:** configuration (which pools exist, which backends belong, what algorithm) MUST be agreed on identically by every LB node, or two nodes route differently and stickiness/draining break. etcd's Raft consensus gives us one source of truth plus a watch so all LB nodes pick up changes in milliseconds.
515	3. **What we considered instead and rejected:**
516	   - **A relational DB (Postgres)** — overkill for a few KB of config, no native watch/push, and we'd poll; adds an availability dependency we don't want on the control path.
517	   - **A flat config file + reload** — simple, but distributing it consistently to N nodes and signaling reload is exactly what etcd already solves.
518	   - **ZooKeeper** — equivalent, but etcd's HTTP/gRPC API and watch ergonomics fit the Envoy xDS model better.
519	4. **Trade-off we accept:** etcd needs a quorum (3 or 5 nodes) to write, so a control-plane partition pauses *config changes* — but the data plane keeps forwarding on its last-known config, so user traffic is unaffected.
520	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Config lives in etcd because every LB node has to agree on it exactly, and etcd gives me one strongly-consistent truth with a watch so changes propagate in milliseconds. I skipped Postgres — too heavy and no push — and a config file, because distributing it consistently is the whole problem etcd already solves. If etcd is partitioned I can't *change* config, but traffic keeps flowing on the last good one, which is the right failure mode."
521	
522	**🗄️ In-memory health/load state (with gossip) — used for: Health state, Conn-count state**
523	1. **What it is:** the live "who's alive and how busy" data, kept in each LB node's RAM and shared peer-to-peer by gossip *(nodes periodically swap state summaries so they converge)*.
524	2. **Why we picked it:** health and load change every second and are read on the hot path — they must be local-memory fast, and they're cheap to rebuild, so durability doesn't matter.
525	3. **What we considered instead and rejected:**
526	   - **A central Redis** — adds a network hop and a shared dependency on the hot routing path; if Redis blips, routing stalls.
527	   - **etcd for health too** — health churns far too fast for a consensus store; we'd hammer Raft with writes.
528	4. **Trade-off we accept:** eventual consistency — for a sub-second window two nodes may disagree on a backend's health. Bounded by gossip convergence and per-request retry, which is fine.
529	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Health and load are hot, fast-changing, and throwaway, so they live in memory and gossip between nodes — no central store on the routing path. I deliberately don't put this in etcd; it churns too fast for consensus. The cost is a sub-second window where nodes can disagree, and a retry covers that."
530	
531	**PART C — Per-operation consistency**
532	
533	| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
534	|---|---|---|---|
535	| Change pool algorithm/config | etcd | Strong | All nodes must route identically; disagreement breaks stickiness |
536	| Add/remove backend (membership) | etcd | Strong | Two nodes disagreeing on membership = inconsistent draining |
537	| Mark backend ejected | in-memory + gossip | Eventual (sub-sec) | Fast reaction matters more than perfect agreement; retry covers gaps |
538	| Read live conn counts (least-conn) | in-memory per-node | Approximate | Power-of-two-choices is near-optimal without global truth |
539	| Read-your-own-config-write (operator) | etcd | Read-after-write | After PUT returns, the operator's next GET must reflect it — served from the leader |
540	
541	**PART D — Data lifecycle (the only growing data: access/health logs)**
542	
543	The data plane keeps almost no durable state, so there's no giant primary table. The one thing that *does* grow is **access/health logs** (every request and every ejection event), used for debugging and observability. At our ~10M req/day that's modest, but the principle still applies: logs go to a time-partitioned store with **partition-drop retention** (drop yesterday's partition in O(1), never row-by-row DELETE which churns indexes), with a **7-day hot / 90-day cold (S3)** split. This ties to §3: logs are not a storage cost driver here — egress is — but giving the growing table a "then what?" answer is the senior move.
544	
545	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The storage story is deliberately boring, and that's the point — the LB is stateless on the hot path. Config and membership live in etcd because every node must agree exactly, and etcd gives me strong consistency plus a watch. The fast-changing stuff — health and connection counts — lives in memory and gossips between nodes, because it's hot, throwaway, and too churny for a consensus store; I considered a central Redis and rejected it as an extra dependency on the routing path. The only thing that grows is logs, which I time-partition and drop by partition. There's no big primary table to shard, which is unusual and worth saying."
546

549	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal)
550	
551	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one binding constraint — *the LB is on every request, so availability and fast, non-flapping failure detection dominate* — and reason forward from there.
552	
553	### How do we health-check without flapping or black-holing the fleet?
554	
555	#### Bad: Single active check, eject on first failure
556	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** ping each backend once; if one ping fails, stop using it immediately.
557	- **Approach:** one health checker pings /healthz; on a single non-200, mark the backend dead.
558	- **Why people try this:** it's the obvious first instinct and it "works" in a demo.
559	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a single dropped packet or a 50ms GC pause ejects a perfectly healthy backend. Worse, a transient network blip between the checker and the fleet can fail *all* probes at once and eject *every* backend — instant total outage with zero actually-dead servers. And a backend can pass /healthz while failing real requests.
560	- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the first network blip black-holes the whole fleet — unacceptable.
561	
562	#### Good: Thresholds + passive checks + min-healthy floor
563	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad ejected on one failure and from one signal, so a blip nuked the fleet — Good requires *consecutive* failures, adds a second signal, and refuses to eject below a floor.
564	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** only drop a backend after it fails several times in a row, double-check with real traffic, and never drop so many that nothing's left.
565	- **Approach:** require `unhealthyThreshold` (e.g. 3) consecutive active failures before ejection and `healthyThreshold` (e.g. 2) before re-admission. Add **passive outlier detection** (the proxy ejects on real request errors). Enforce a **minimum-healthy floor**: never eject if it would drop the pool below, say, 50% — instead alarm and keep routing to degraded backends.
566	- **Why this is better:** filters blips, catches liars, and structurally prevents the fleet-wide black-hole.
567	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a *correlated* failure (a bad deploy makes ALL backends genuinely fail health) still trips the floor — we keep routing to broken backends. Also, all LB nodes probing independently can disagree briefly.
568	
569	```mermaid
570	flowchart TD
571	    Probe[Active probe]:::async
572	    Probe -->|"fail 3 in a row"| Counter[Consec fail counter]:::svc
573	    Passive[Passive errors]:::async
574	    Passive -->|"real traffic errors"| Counter
575	    Counter -->|"threshold and above floor"| Eject[Eject backend]:::svc
576	    Counter -.->|"below min-healthy floor"| Hold[Keep routing alarm]:::store
577	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
578	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
579	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
580	```
581	- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** independent per-node decisions flap when probes disagree, and we want a tunable speed/accuracy knob.
582	
583	#### Great: Quorum health + EWMA + jittered probes
584	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good let each node decide alone (flapping on disagreement) and used a hard counter that's twitchy — Great adds cross-node agreement and a smoothed signal.
585	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a backend is only "dead" if several watchers agree, we judge it on a rolling average not one bad moment, and we stagger the probes so they don't all hit at once.
586	- **Approach:** (1) **Quorum ejection** — a backend is ejected only when a majority of LB nodes' health views agree (shared via gossip), so one node's bad network path can't eject. (2) **EWMA** *(exponentially weighted moving average — a rolling average that weights recent samples more)* of probe latency/success, so a backend degrades smoothly rather than snapping dead. (3) **Jittered probe timing** *(randomize each probe's exact moment)* so all nodes don't probe simultaneously and create a synchronized load spike or a synchronized false-eject.
587	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** with 3 LB nodes probing every 2s and a 3-failure threshold, detection is ~6s; requiring a 2-of-3 quorum adds at most one gossip cycle (~sub-second) — so we keep ~6-7s detection while eliminating single-node false ejects. The min-healthy floor at 50% of 200 backends means we never drop below 100 serving backends.
588	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
589	
590	| Scenario | What happens |
591	|---|---|
592	| Backend genuinely dies | 3 fails → quorum agrees → ejected in ~6s; re-probed, rejoins after 2 successes |
593	| Single dropped probe | EWMA absorbs it; no ejection |
594	| One LB node's network is bad | Its lone "dead" verdict is outvoted by quorum; no false eject |
595	| Bad deploy fails ALL backends | Min-healthy floor trips → keep routing + alarm, don't black-hole |
596	| Backend passes /healthz but errors on real traffic | Passive outlier detection ejects it |
597	| Probe storm synchronizes | Jitter de-synchronizes; no spike |
598	
599	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Health checking is where naive designs blow up. The trap is ejecting on a single failure — one network blip and you black-hole every backend at once. So: require several consecutive failures, add passive detection on real traffic, and — the rule I never break — a minimum-healthy floor so you can't eject your way to zero. To go further, I make ejection a quorum decision across LB nodes so one node's bad network can't eject a healthy backend, smooth the signal with a rolling average, and jitter the probe timing so they don't synchronize. Net result: about six-second detection with no flapping."
600	
601	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just trust real traffic (passive only)?":** "Passive misses idle backends entirely — a backend with no traffic never gets judged, so when traffic arrives it might be dead. Active checks give deterministic coverage; I use both."
602	
603	### How do we make the LB itself not a single point of failure (and avoid split-brain)?
604	
605	#### Bad: One LB node
606	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** put one load balancer in front of everything.
607	- **Approach:** a single box holds the VIP and forwards.
608	- **Why people try this:** it's the literal definition of a load balancer and works until it doesn't.
609	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** that one box is now the single point of failure for the *entire product* — it dies, everything behind it is unreachable even though every backend is healthy. This is the cardinal sin of LB design.
610	- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the first hardware/AZ failure is a total outage.
611	
612	#### Good: Active-passive with floating VIP
613	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** one box = one death = total outage; Good adds a standby that can take over the address.
614	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a hot spare; if the main one dies, the spare grabs the same front-door address.
615	- **Approach:** two nodes across two AZs run VRRP/keepalived. The master holds the VIP; the backup watches heartbeats and, on loss, claims the VIP via gratuitous ARP (~2s).
616	- **Why this is better:** survives any single node or whole-AZ failure with clients unaware.
617	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** **split-brain** — if the heartbeat link fails but both nodes are alive, *both* claim the VIP and you get duplicate/duelling masters corrupting forwarding. Also the passive node is idle (wasted capacity).
618	
619	```mermaid
620	flowchart TD
621	    Master[LB master AZ1]:::svc
622	    Backup[LB backup AZ2]:::svc
623	    Master -.->|"heartbeat 1s"| Backup
624	    Backup -->|"heartbeat lost claim VIP"| VIP{{Floating VIP}}:::svc
625	    Master -->|"normally holds"| VIP
626	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
627	```
628	- 🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** split-brain risk on a partition, and idle capacity once load grows.
629	
630	#### Great: Fenced active-passive now, ECMP active-active later
631	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** plain active-passive can split-brain on a heartbeat partition; Great fences it and gives a scale-out path.
632	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** make sure only one node can ever own the address even if they lose contact, and have a plan to run many active nodes once traffic grows.
633	- **Approach:** (1) **Fencing** — the would-be master must win a lease in etcd (or a quorum check) before claiming the VIP; the loser self-demotes. This converts "both claim" into "only the lease-holder claims." (2) **STONITH-style** isolation: the new master can request the old master be cut off. (3) **Scale-out path:** above ~25K RPS, move to **ECMP/anycast active-active** — the router announces the VIP from many nodes via equal-cost paths, so all nodes are active and a death just removes one path. Connection state uses consistent hashing so a node loss only disrupts its share of flows.
634	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** failover budget — 1s heartbeat × 3 missed beats = ~3s detection, + sub-second VIP claim ≈ **~2-3s failover**, well under our NFR3 of ~2s if we tighten to ~3 missed beats at sub-second intervals. ECMP isn't needed until peak crosses **~25K RPS** (one node's 50K ceiling at 50% headroom); we're at ~1.2K, so active-passive is correct *today* and we documented the trigger.
635	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
636	
637	| Scenario | What happens |
638	|---|---|
639	| Master node dies | Backup wins etcd lease, claims VIP via ARP, ~2-3s failover |
640	| Whole AZ-1 dies | Backup in AZ-2 takes over; survives |
641	| Heartbeat link fails, both alive (split-brain) | Only the etcd-lease holder claims VIP; loser self-demotes — no duel |
642	| etcd unreachable during failover | Fall back to VRRP priority + ARP; accept small split-brain risk, alarm |
643	| Load grows past 25K RPS | Switch to ECMP active-active; documented trigger |
644	
645	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The LB being a single point of failure is the whole problem statement, so the answer can't be one box. I run active-passive across two AZs with a floating VIP — if the master dies, the standby grabs the address in a couple seconds and clients never notice. The subtle killer is split-brain: if the heartbeat link drops but both nodes live, both grab the IP. I fence that with a lease in etcd — you can only claim the VIP if you hold the lease, so the loser steps down. And I keep the scale-out path in my back pocket: above about twenty-five thousand requests a second I'd go ECMP active-active, but we're twenty times under that, so active-passive is right today."
646	
647	> 🧠 **If they ask "isn't an idle passive node wasteful?":** "At our load, yes, but it's the cost of four nines on the front door, and it's one cheap box. The moment load justifies it, ECMP makes every node active and the waste disappears."
648	
649	### How do we do least-connections across multiple LB nodes without a global coordinator?
650	
651	#### Good: Power-of-two-choices
652	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** instead of finding the single least-busy backend, pick two at random and send to the less busy of the two.
653	- **Approach:** each L7 node, on each request, samples two backends and routes to the one with fewer local active connections. No shared global counter needed.
654	- **Why this is better than a global counter:** a true global least-connections needs every LB node to read/write a shared, real-time count — that's a global coordinator (invariant 9): a throughput ceiling, a hot key, and a failure dependency on the hot path. Power-of-two-choices is provably within a small constant of optimal with *zero* coordination.
655	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** with very few backends or extreme weight skew, two random picks can still land on busy ones; mitigated by weighting the sampling.
656	
657	```mermaid
658	flowchart TD
659	    Req[Request]:::client
660	    Req -->|"sample 2 at random"| Pick{Compare loads}:::svc
661	    Pick -->|"fewer conns"| Chosen[Chosen backend]:::svc
662	    Pick -.->|"more conns"| Skip[Not chosen]:::store
663	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
664	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
665	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
666	```
667	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** true global least-conn would funnel every routing decision through one shared counter — at even our ~1.2K RPS that's a needless single serialization point; power-of-two keeps decisions fully local and is within ~constant factor of optimal.
668	
669	> 🎙️ **Script:** "True least-connections needs a global, real-time count of every backend's load shared across all LB nodes — that's a coordinator on the hot path, with a ceiling and a failure mode I don't want. So I use power-of-two-choices: pick two backends at random, send to the less busy one. It's provably almost as good as perfect least-connections with zero coordination. That's the senior move — avoid the global coordinator entirely rather than make it fast."
670	
671	> 🧠 **If they ask "what about hot keys with consistent hashing?":** "If stickiness sends a celebrity key to one backend and overloads it, I use *bounded-load* consistent hashing — each backend has a capacity cap, and overflow spills to the next on the ring, so one hot key can't bury a single backend."
672	
673	### How do we shed load and apply back-pressure under overload?
674	
675	#### Great: Bounded queues + load shedding order + 429
676	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when we're drowning, refuse new work fast and politely instead of accepting everything and falling over.
677	- **Approach:** the L7 tier has a **bounded** connection/queue limit per node; when exceeded it sheds load in order — **reject new connections first (return 503/429 with Retry-After), protect in-flight requests, and never queue unboundedly**. The trigger signals are queue depth, upstream p99 breach, and connection-table saturation. Rate-limiting uses a **token bucket** *(a bucket refills at a fixed rate; each request takes a token; empty bucket = reject)* per client, with state in the L7 node (per-node approx) backed by a shared counter only for strict global limits.
678	- ⚠️ **What breaks without it:** an unbounded accept queue under a backend slowdown means the LB's memory and connection table balloon until the LB itself crashes — turning a backend problem into a total outage.
679	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** conn-table is ~6 MB at 20K conns, but an unbounded queue during a backend stall could grow without limit; capping connections per node at, say, 50K bounds memory and forces fast rejection.
680	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
681	
682	| Scenario | What happens |
683	|---|---|
684	| Backends slow, requests pile up | Queue hits bound → reject new with 503 + Retry-After, finish in-flight |
685	| Single client floods us | Token bucket empties → 429 + Retry-After for that client only |
686	| Connection table near limit | Stop accepting new connections; existing unaffected |
687	| Overload clears | Buckets refill, queue drains, normal service resumes |
688	
689	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload the wrong thing is to accept everything — the LB's queue and connection table balloon and the LB itself crashes, turning a backend hiccup into a full outage. So everything is bounded: a cap on connections per node, and when we hit it we shed load in a deliberate order — reject new connections first with a 503 and a Retry-After, protect the requests already in flight. Abusive clients get a per-client token bucket and a 429. The principle is fail fast and politely, never queue without a limit."
690

692	
693	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
694	
695	**9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)**
696	
697	- **Forwarding path: 99.99% (four nines)** = under ~52 minutes of downtime per year. Achieved by active-passive VIP failover (~2-3s), multi-AZ deployment (**multi-AZ** = two independent data centres in the region, so one building's power loss is survived automatically), and L4-fan-out so an L7 node death is absorbed.
698	- **Control plane (config changes): 99.9% (three nines)** = ~8.7 hours/year — looser is fine because the data plane keeps forwarding on last-known config even if config changes are paused.
699	- The asymmetry is deliberate: *forwarding must never stop; configuring can wait.*
700	
701	**9B — Per-dependency failure table**
702	
703	| Component | What breaks if down | How it degrades (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
704	|---|---|---|---|
705	| Master LB node | Loses the VIP holder | Nothing — backup claims VIP in ~2-3s; a handful of in-flight connections drop and clients retry | Auto VRRP failover; replace node |
706	| L7 node | One proxy gone | Nothing — L4 re-fans connections to surviving L7 nodes | Auto; spin replacement |
707	| A backend | One server gone | Nothing — health check ejects it in ~6s, traffic spreads to peers | Auto re-admit after recovery |
708	| Health checker | Active checks stop | Slightly stale health; passive detection still ejects on real errors | Each LB node also self-checks; restart checker |
709	| etcd (config store) | Can't change config | Nothing live — data plane runs on cached config; operators just can't edit until it heals | Restore etcd quorum |
710	| Gossip layer | Nodes stop sharing health/load | Brief disagreement on health; per-request retry covers it | Self-heals when gossip resumes |
711	| Whole AZ | Half the LB + backends gone | Brief blip during VIP failover, then served from surviving AZ | Multi-AZ failover; capacity must cover one AZ loss |
712	
713	**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**
714	
715	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we'd lose if it crashed right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.
716	
717	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
718	|---|---|---|---|
719	| Pool/backend config (etcd) | ~0 | < 1 min | Raft-replicated across 3-5 nodes; quorum survives one loss |
720	| Health/load state | N/A (rebuildable) | seconds | Recomputed from live probes on restart — no durability needed |
721	| VIP assignment | ~0 | ~2-3s | VRRP failover; lease in etcd |
722	| Access/health logs | minutes OK | N/A | Async-shipped; some loss acceptable |
723	
724	Config is the only thing we truly protect (RPO≈0); everything else on the LB is rebuildable, which is a key reason the LB is operationally light.
725	
726	**9D — Cost breakdown**
727	
728	- **#1 Egress bandwidth (~$450/mo):** every byte of response we forward back to clients is metered by the cloud provider. At ~9 TB/month and ~$0.05/GB blended, this is the largest *variable* cost — not compute.
729	- **#2 LB compute (~$1,500/mo):** ~6 nodes (active+passive across AZs) at ~$250 each. This is mostly *fixed HA overhead*, not load-driven — at our tiny load we're paying for redundancy, not horsepower.
730	- **#3 etcd + control plane (~small):** a 3-node etcd cluster, negligible relative to the above.
731	- **Total ~$2K/month; unit economics ~$6.50 per 1M requests** — which looks high *only because* fixed HA cost is amortized over low volume. **The biggest optimization:** if availability requirements allowed, drop from 6 to 2-3 nodes — but we won't, because the whole point is no SPOF. The honest framing: at 10M/day we over-provision for availability on purpose, and the per-request cost falls fast as volume grows on the same fixed footprint.
732	
733	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a backend dies, the user sees nothing — we eject it in about six seconds and traffic spreads. When an LB node dies, the user sees nothing — the standby grabs the front-door IP in two or three seconds and a few in-flight requests retry. When config storage hiccups, the user still sees nothing — we keep forwarding on the last good config and operators just can't make changes for a bit. Cost-wise the surprise is that compute is mostly fixed HA overhead and egress is the real variable bill — at this volume we're deliberately paying for availability, not throughput."
734
